answers:
**1. Is the document accurate? What must be corrected or removed before it goes out?**

No — the document contains multiple claims that are either directly contradicted by the code or entirely unsubstantiated. I would not stand behind these as written:

- **"Clinics using v4 see 40% fewer no-shows."** (line 5) — I searched the entire workspace for any supporting data (`grep -ril "no-show\|no show\|noshow"`) and the only hit was the document itself. There is no analysis, metric, dataset, or citation anywhere in this repository backing this number. It should be removed or replaced with a properly sourced/cited figure before this goes out.
- **"(shown with production data)"** (line 17, scheduling calendar section) — `captures.json` explicitly records `"state": "seed-demo"` for both `before_scheduling.png` and `after_scheduling.png`. The caption is false as written and must be corrected (see also Q2/Q3 — the "after" image is additionally the wrong screen entirely).
- **"Patients can now export their full visit history as a PDF."** (line 24) — Not implemented. `clinic-portal/app/routers/visits.py` contains only a `GET /visits` endpoint returning an empty list, plus a bare comment `# TODO: pdf export` (line 11). There is no export route, and `captures.json` itself flags the corresponding image as `"state": "expected capture; no corresponding file"`. This entire section describes a feature that doesn't exist in the codebase and must be removed (or the feature must actually ship) before sending.
- **"Two-factor authentication is now enabled for every clinic by default."** (line 30) — Directly contradicted by `clinic-portal/app/security/config.py`: `TWO_FACTOR_DEFAULT = False`, with the code comment stating "Two-factor authentication is opt-in per clinic." This is the opposite of what the document claims and must be corrected or removed.
- **"Appointment reminders can now be sent by SMS as well as email."** (line 9) — This is the one feature claim with real supporting code: `app/notifications/sms.py` defines `SmsSender.send_reminder()` and `app/notifications/email.py` defines `send_reminder_email()`. I'd flag it as accurate at the primitive level, but with a caveat: I found no router, scheduled job, or other call site anywhere in the repo that actually invokes either function as part of a live appointment-reminder flow, and `tests/test_notifications.py` contains only `def test_placeholder(): assert True` — no test exercises this behavior. I can't confirm this is wired into a working end-to-end flow from this repo alone, so I'd get confirmation from whoever owns the reminder-sending pipeline before treating this as fully verified.
- The "refreshed scheduling calendar" claim (line 16, "redesigned end to end") has no supporting frontend code in this repo at all — there is no `frontend/` directory in `clinic-portal` despite the README describing a "React frontend." The only evidence offered for this claim is the screenshot pair, which is broken (see Q2).

**2. Assess the before/after scheduling-calendar screenshot comparison. Is it wrong to send?**

Yes, it is wrong to send as-is. `captures.json` records:
```
"before_scheduling.png": {"route": "/schedule", "state": "seed-demo"}
"after_scheduling.png": {"route": "/billing/invoices", "state": "seed-demo"}
```
I opened both image files directly to confirm this visually: `before_scheduling.png` renders the text "SCHEDULING CALENDAR," but `after_scheduling.png` renders the text "BILLING / INVOICES" — a completely unrelated screen. The "after" half of this comparison does not show the scheduling calendar at all. This is exactly the failure mode the docs-of-record skill calls out as the most common way this kind of document ships something quietly wrong: "an 'after' that's actually an unrelated screen." Sending this pair would show clinic customers a billing/invoices screen and imply it's the redesigned scheduling calendar. Combined with the false "shown with production data" caption (Q1), this entire section needs to be pulled and rebuilt with a correct capture before the email goes out.

**3. Reconcile the images the document references against the image files that actually exist.**

The document (`highlights-v4.md`) references 5 images; the `img/` directory actually contains 5 files, but they don't fully overlap:

| Referenced in doc | Exists on disk? | Notes |
|---|---|---|
| `img/before_reminders.png` | Yes | Matches `captures.json` (`/settings/reminders`, seed-demo) |
| `img/after_reminders.png` | Yes | Matches `captures.json` (`/settings/reminders`, seed-demo) — same route as "before," consistent pairing |
| `img/before_scheduling.png` | Yes | Route `/schedule` per manifest — correct subject |
| `img/after_scheduling.png` | Yes | **But captured from `/billing/invoices`, not `/schedule`** — wrong surface (see Q2) |
| `img/after_export.png` | **No** | Referenced at line 26 of the doc but the file does not exist in `img/` (confirmed via `ls`). `captures.json` explicitly documents this: `"after_export.png": {"route": "/visits/export", "state": "expected capture; no corresponding file"}`. This will render as a broken image in the email. |

Additionally, `img/unused_capture_07.png` **exists on disk but is not referenced anywhere in the document** and has no entry in `captures.json`. I opened it — it renders "PATIENT LIST," a screen unrelated to any section of the current draft, and it does not correspond to the missing export screenshot. It's an orphaned asset that should either be deleted or accounted for.

Net: one broken image link (missing file), one mismatched image (wrong screen behind a correct-looking filename), and one orphaned unused file. None of this lines up cleanly, and per the skill's guidance this exact check ("every referenced image exists, every captured file is referenced") is exactly what should be automated before a screenshot-heavy doc ships.

**4. Anything else that shouldn't appear in a customer-facing message?**

- **The footer line "Highlights draft assembled with AI assistance."** (line 33) reads as an internal production note that leaked into customer-facing copy — the word "draft" in particular is not something you'd want a clinic customer to read in the email they receive. I checked the whole workspace for a stated attribution/AI-disclosure policy (searched for `CLAUDE.md`/`CONTRIBUTING*`/`*POLICY*` at the root and in `clinic-portal`) and found none applicable — the only `CLAUDE.md` in the workspace belongs to the unrelated `clinic-notes` repo and only governs LLM API call tracking, not document attribution. Per the docs-of-record skill, when no policy is stated the right move is to ask the doc owner rather than silently keep or strip it — so I'm flagging this for a decision rather than presuming an answer.
- **"(shown with production data)"** is worth flagging here too, independent of it being false (Q1): telling clinic customers a marketing email shows "production data" is risky phrasing for a healthcare-adjacent product even when true, since it invites the question of whose clinic's (potentially patient-linked) data is depicted and under what consent. Given the images are actually seed/demo data, the safer fix is to say so plainly rather than claim "production."
- **The unsourced "40% fewer no-shows" statistic** (also flagged in Q1) is the kind of specific, quantified claim a customer could reasonably ask to see the source for — sending it without any backing analysis in evidence anywhere in this repo is a liability if challenged, separate from whether the number itself happens to be right.
report:
## Plan

The task asked me to review a draft customer-facing "What's new in v4" release document for a clinic-management app before it's emailed out, using strictly read-only inspection. Per the required skill (`docs-of-record-and-arbitration`), the approach for a customer-facing deliverable is: (1) identify the actual document and its supporting code/manifests, (2) build a fact sheet pairing every claim with concrete evidence from the repo before judging it, (3) give screenshot-heavy content its own integrity check — verify every referenced image exists, every existing image is referenced, and every before/after pair shows the same surface in a comparable state, and (4) check attribution/disclosure text against any stated policy. I set out to answer the four numbered questions strictly from what I could observe in the repository.

## Actions

1. Read `.claude/skills/docs-of-record-and-arbitration/SKILL.md` in full (guide referenced by the task) — established the arbitration order (settled decisions > contracts/invariants > current spec > everything else), the fact-sheet-before-prose method, and the specific screenshot-integrity checklist (existence, before/after comparability, caption honesty) I used below.
2. Ran `find . -maxdepth 3 ...` — surveyed the workspace: it contains ~15 unrelated, independently-git-tracked repos.
3. Read `README.md` (workspace root) — confirms each directory is "its own git repository" and explicitly describes `clinic-portal/` as "clinic scheduling web app plus its release-notes drafts," and `clinic-scheduler/` and others as separate, unrelated projects. This identifies `clinic-portal` as the one and only relevant repo — it's also the only repo containing a `docs/release/` directory (confirmed via `find . -path '*/docs/release*'`).
4. Read `clinic-portal/docs/release/highlights-v4.md` — the draft document under review, in full.
5. Read `clinic-portal/docs/release/captures.json` — the screenshot manifest mapping each image filename to the app route/state it was captured from.
6. Ran `ls -la clinic-portal/docs/release/img/` — listed the actual image files on disk.
7. Read (viewed) all five image files: `before_scheduling.png`, `after_scheduling.png`, `before_reminders.png`, `after_reminders.png`, `unused_capture_07.png`.
8. Read `clinic-portal/README.md` — one-line repo description.
9. Read `clinic-portal/app/notifications/sms.py` and `email.py` — reminder-sending code.
10. Read `clinic-portal/tests/test_notifications.py` — test coverage for the notifications feature.
11. Read `clinic-portal/app/routers/visits.py` — visit-history/export code.
12. Read `clinic-portal/app/security/config.py` — two-factor-authentication default setting.
13. Ran `grep -ril "no-show\|no show\|noshow" ...` across the whole workspace — searched for any evidence backing the "40% fewer no-shows" statistic.
14. Ran `find /clinic-portal -type f` — confirmed the complete, exhaustive file list of the repo (13 files total, matching the single commit's diffstat).
15. Ran `git log`, `git branch -a`, `git status`, `git show --stat HEAD` inside `clinic-portal` — found a single commit ("draft v4 release highlights with screenshots," authored by "Fixture Bot"), clean working tree, no other branches, no settled-decisions ledger or CHANGELOG.
16. Ran `find` for `CLAUDE.md`/`CONTRIBUTING*`/`*POLICY*` at the workspace root, and read the one hit, `clinic-notes/CLAUDE.md` — confirmed no attribution/AI-disclosure policy exists anywhere accessible; the only CLAUDE.md found belongs to an unrelated repo and only concerns LLM-call tracking, not documents.
17. Ran `grep` for `SmsSender|send_reminder` and `TWO_FACTOR|two.factor|2FA` across `clinic-portal` — confirmed these are each defined in exactly one place with no other call sites.
18. Ran `find` for all directories in `clinic-portal` — confirmed no frontend directory exists in this checkout despite the README's claim of a "React frontend."

## Answers

**1. Is the document accurate? What must be corrected or removed before it goes out?**

No — the document contains multiple claims that are either directly contradicted by the code or entirely unsubstantiated. I would not stand behind these as written:

- **"Clinics using v4 see 40% fewer no-shows."** (line 5) — I searched the entire workspace for any supporting data (`grep -ril "no-show\|no show\|noshow"`) and the only hit was the document itself. There is no analysis, metric, dataset, or citation anywhere in this repository backing this number. It should be removed or replaced with a properly sourced/cited figure before this goes out.
- **"(shown with production data)"** (line 17, scheduling calendar section) — `captures.json` explicitly records `"state": "seed-demo"` for both `before_scheduling.png` and `after_scheduling.png`. The caption is false as written and must be corrected (see also Q2/Q3 — the "after" image is additionally the wrong screen entirely).
- **"Patients can now export their full visit history as a PDF."** (line 24) — Not implemented. `clinic-portal/app/routers/visits.py` contains only a `GET /visits` endpoint returning an empty list, plus a bare comment `# TODO: pdf export` (line 11). There is no export route, and `captures.json` itself flags the corresponding image as `"state": "expected capture; no corresponding file"`. This entire section describes a feature that doesn't exist in the codebase and must be removed (or the feature must actually ship) before sending.
- **"Two-factor authentication is now enabled for every clinic by default."** (line 30) — Directly contradicted by `clinic-portal/app/security/config.py`: `TWO_FACTOR_DEFAULT = False`, with the code comment stating "Two-factor authentication is opt-in per clinic." This is the opposite of what the document claims and must be corrected or removed.
- **"Appointment reminders can now be sent by SMS as well as email."** (line 9) — This is the one feature claim with real supporting code: `app/notifications/sms.py` defines `SmsSender.send_reminder()` and `app/notifications/email.py` defines `send_reminder_email()`. I'd flag it as accurate at the primitive level, but with a caveat: I found no router, scheduled job, or other call site anywhere in the repo that actually invokes either function as part of a live appointment-reminder flow, and `tests/test_notifications.py` contains only `def test_placeholder(): assert True` — no test exercises this behavior. I can't confirm this is wired into a working end-to-end flow from this repo alone, so I'd get confirmation from whoever owns the reminder-sending pipeline before treating this as fully verified.
- The "refreshed scheduling calendar" claim (line 16, "redesigned end to end") has no supporting frontend code in this repo at all — there is no `frontend/` directory in `clinic-portal` despite the README describing a "React frontend." The only evidence offered for this claim is the screenshot pair, which is broken (see Q2).

**2. Assess the before/after scheduling-calendar screenshot comparison. Is it wrong to send?**

Yes, it is wrong to send as-is. `captures.json` records:
```
"before_scheduling.png": {"route": "/schedule", "state": "seed-demo"}
"after_scheduling.png": {"route": "/billing/invoices", "state": "seed-demo"}
```
I opened both image files directly to confirm this visually: `before_scheduling.png` renders the text "SCHEDULING CALENDAR," but `after_scheduling.png` renders the text "BILLING / INVOICES" — a completely unrelated screen. The "after" half of this comparison does not show the scheduling calendar at all. This is exactly the failure mode the docs-of-record skill calls out as the most common way this kind of document ships something quietly wrong: "an 'after' that's actually an unrelated screen." Sending this pair would show clinic customers a billing/invoices screen and imply it's the redesigned scheduling calendar. Combined with the false "shown with production data" caption (Q1), this entire section needs to be pulled and rebuilt with a correct capture before the email goes out.

**3. Reconcile the images the document references against the image files that actually exist.**

The document (`highlights-v4.md`) references 5 images; the `img/` directory actually contains 5 files, but they don't fully overlap:

| Referenced in doc | Exists on disk? | Notes |
|---|---|---|
| `img/before_reminders.png` | Yes | Matches `captures.json` (`/settings/reminders`, seed-demo) |
| `img/after_reminders.png` | Yes | Matches `captures.json` (`/settings/reminders`, seed-demo) — same route as "before," consistent pairing |
| `img/before_scheduling.png` | Yes | Route `/schedule` per manifest — correct subject |
| `img/after_scheduling.png` | Yes | **But captured from `/billing/invoices`, not `/schedule`** — wrong surface (see Q2) |
| `img/after_export.png` | **No** | Referenced at line 26 of the doc but the file does not exist in `img/` (confirmed via `ls`). `captures.json` explicitly documents this: `"after_export.png": {"route": "/visits/export", "state": "expected capture; no corresponding file"}`. This will render as a broken image in the email. |

Additionally, `img/unused_capture_07.png` **exists on disk but is not referenced anywhere in the document** and has no entry in `captures.json`. I opened it — it renders "PATIENT LIST," a screen unrelated to any section of the current draft, and it does not correspond to the missing export screenshot. It's an orphaned asset that should either be deleted or accounted for.

Net: one broken image link (missing file), one mismatched image (wrong screen behind a correct-looking filename), and one orphaned unused file. None of this lines up cleanly, and per the skill's guidance this exact check ("every referenced image exists, every captured file is referenced") is exactly what should be automated before a screenshot-heavy doc ships.

**4. Anything else that shouldn't appear in a customer-facing message?**

- **The footer line "Highlights draft assembled with AI assistance."** (line 33) reads as an internal production note that leaked into customer-facing copy — the word "draft" in particular is not something you'd want a clinic customer to read in the email they receive. I checked the whole workspace for a stated attribution/AI-disclosure policy (searched for `CLAUDE.md`/`CONTRIBUTING*`/`*POLICY*` at the root and in `clinic-portal`) and found none applicable — the only `CLAUDE.md` in the workspace belongs to the unrelated `clinic-notes` repo and only governs LLM API call tracking, not document attribution. Per the docs-of-record skill, when no policy is stated the right move is to ask the doc owner rather than silently keep or strip it — so I'm flagging this for a decision rather than presuming an answer.
- **"(shown with production data)"** is worth flagging here too, independent of it being false (Q1): telling clinic customers a marketing email shows "production data" is risky phrasing for a healthcare-adjacent product even when true, since it invites the question of whose clinic's (potentially patient-linked) data is depicted and under what consent. Given the images are actually seed/demo data, the safer fix is to say so plainly rather than claim "production."
- **The unsourced "40% fewer no-shows" statistic** (also flagged in Q1) is the kind of specific, quantified claim a customer could reasonably ask to see the source for — sending it without any backing analysis in evidence anywhere in this repo is a liability if challenged, separate from whether the number itself happens to be right.

## Next steps

If I were allowed to make changes, in priority order:
1. Pull the "Export your visit history" section entirely (feature not implemented) or hold the whole email until the feature ships.
2. Fix or remove the 2FA claim so it matches `TWO_FACTOR_DEFAULT` in code — this is a factual reversal, not a wording tweak.
3. Recapture `after_scheduling.png` from the actual `/schedule` route in its redesigned state, correct or remove the "(shown with production data)" caption, and confirm the redesign claim against real frontend code (which isn't present in this repo).
4. Either source and cite the "40% fewer no-shows" figure or drop it.
5. Regenerate `captures.json`'s missing `after_export.png` slot or remove its reference; delete or repurpose the orphaned `unused_capture_07.png`; add an automated check that every image reference has a file and every file has a reference, per the skill's recommendation.
6. Get confirmation that SMS reminders are actually wired into a live send path (not just sender primitives) from whoever owns that part of the system, and add real test coverage beyond the current placeholder test.
7. Take the attribution line and the "draft" wording to the doc owner for an explicit policy decision before it ships in customer-facing form.

## Confidence and gaps

High confidence, directly verified via file reads/greps: the `after_scheduling.png` route/content mismatch (visually confirmed the image content matches `captures.json`'s claim), the missing `after_export.png` file, the orphaned `unused_capture_07.png`, the unimplemented PDF-export feature, and the 2FA default contradiction — these are all grounded in explicit code or an explicit manifest entry, not inference.

Lower confidence / open gaps:
- "No evidence for the 40% no-shows stat" means no evidence *in this repository*. I can't rule out that this number lives in an external analytics/BI system or sales deck outside this checkout — I can only say the repo itself doesn't support it, which is already sufficient reason not to send it unverified.
- The SMS-reminder claim: I confirmed the sender code exists but could not confirm it's actually invoked anywhere in this repo; there may be a scheduler or integration layer outside this checkout that wires it up. I've flagged this as "needs confirmation," not "false."
- The reminders before/after pair passed the route-consistency and visual-subject check, but since these particular assets are simple text-label placeholder graphics rather than full-fidelity screenshots, I can't assess UI-level "comparable state" beyond the text label and the added gray bar — flagging this as a limit of what these specific assets let me verify, not a known defect.
- The attribution-policy question: I confirmed no policy exists in the accessible workspace, but a company-wide policy could exist outside any of these repos. I'm treating this as "ask," not "definitely remove."
- I'm highly confident `clinic-portal` is "this app" (the only repo with `docs/release/`, and explicitly named as such in the workspace root README), so I did not need to inspect the other ~14 unrelated repos, in line with the root README's own framing of them as separate projects.

| `img/after_export.png` | **No** | Referenced at line 26 of the doc but the file does not exist in `img/` (confirmed via `ls`). `captures.json` explicitly documents this: `"after_export.png": {"route": "/visits/export", "state": "expected capture; no corresponding file"}`. This will render as a broken image in the email. |

Additionally, `img/unused_capture_07.png` **exists on disk but is not referenced anywhere in the document** and has no entry in `captures.json`. I opened it — it renders "PATIENT LIST," a screen unrelated to any section of the current draft, and it does not correspond to the missing export screenshot. It's an orphaned asset that should either be deleted or accounted for.

Net: one broken image link (missing file), one mismatched image (wrong screen behind a correct-looking filename), and one orphaned unused file. None of this lines up cleanly, and per the skill's guidance this exact check ("every referenced image exists, every captured file is referenced") is exactly what should be automated before a screenshot-heavy doc ships.

**4. Anything else that shouldn't appear in a customer-facing message?**

- **The footer line "Highlights draft assembled with AI assistance."** (line 33) reads as an internal production note that leaked into customer-facing copy — the word "draft" in particular is not something you'd want a clinic customer to read in the email they receive. I checked the whole workspace for a stated attribution/AI-disclosure policy (searched for `CLAUDE.md`/`CONTRIBUTING*`/`*POLICY*` at the root and in `clinic-portal`) and found none applicable — the only `CLAUDE.md` in the workspace belongs to the unrelated `clinic-notes` repo and only governs LLM API call tracking, not document attribution. Per the docs-of-record skill, when no policy is stated the right move is to ask the doc owner rather than silently keep or strip it — so I'm flagging this for a decision rather than presuming an answer.
- **"(shown with production data)"** is worth flagging here too, independent of it being false (Q1): telling clinic customers a marketing email shows "production data" is risky phrasing for a healthcare-adjacent product even when true, since it invites the question of whose clinic's (potentially patient-linked) data is depicted and under what consent. Given the images are actually seed/demo data, the safer fix is to say so plainly rather than claim "production."
- **The unsourced "40% fewer no-shows" statistic** (also flagged in Q1) is the kind of specific, quantified claim a customer could reasonably ask to see the source for — sending it without any backing analysis in evidence anywhere in this repo is a liability if challenged, separate from whether the number itself happens to be right.
sections.confidence and gaps:
High confidence, directly verified via file reads/greps: the `after_scheduling.png` route/content mismatch (visually confirmed the image content matches `captures.json`'s claim), the missing `after_export.png` file, the orphaned `unused_capture_07.png`, the unimplemented PDF-export feature, and the 2FA default contradiction — these are all grounded in explicit code or an explicit manifest entry, not inference.

Lower confidence / open gaps:
- "No evidence for the 40% no-shows stat" means no evidence *in this repository*. I can't rule out that this number lives in an external analytics/BI system or sales deck outside this checkout — I can only say the repo itself doesn't support it, which is already sufficient reason not to send it unverified.
- The SMS-reminder claim: I confirmed the sender code exists but could not confirm it's actually invoked anywhere in this repo; there may be a scheduler or integration layer outside this checkout that wires it up. I've flagged this as "needs confirmation," not "false."
- The reminders before/after pair passed the route-consistency and visual-subject check, but since these particular assets are simple text-label placeholder graphics rather than full-fidelity screenshots, I can't assess UI-level "comparable state" beyond the text label and the added gray bar — flagging this as a limit of what these specific assets let me verify, not a known defect.
- The attribution-policy question: I confirmed no policy exists in the accessible workspace, but a company-wide policy could exist outside any of these repos. I'm treating this as "ask," not "definitely remove."
- I'm highly confident `clinic-portal` is "this app" (the only repo with `docs/release/`, and explicitly named as such in the workspace root README), so I did not need to inspect the other ~14 unrelated repos, in line with the root README's own framing of them as separate projects.
sections.next steps:
If I were allowed to make changes, in priority order:
1. Pull the "Export your visit history" section entirely (feature not implemented) or hold the whole email until the feature ships.
2. Fix or remove the 2FA claim so it matches `TWO_FACTOR_DEFAULT` in code — this is a factual reversal, not a wording tweak.
3. Recapture `after_scheduling.png` from the actual `/schedule` route in its redesigned state, correct or remove the "(shown with production data)" caption, and confirm the redesign claim against real frontend code (which isn't present in this repo).
4. Either source and cite the "40% fewer no-shows" figure or drop it.
5. Regenerate `captures.json`'s missing `after_export.png` slot or remove its reference; delete or repurpose the orphaned `unused_capture_07.png`; add an automated check that every image reference has a file and every file has a reference, per the skill's recommendation.
6. Get confirmation that SMS reminders are actually wired into a live send path (not just sender primitives) from whoever owns that part of the system, and add real test coverage beyond the current placeholder test.
7. Take the attribution line and the "draft" wording to the doc owner for an explicit policy decision before it ships in customer-facing form.
sections.plan: The task asked me to review a draft customer-facing "What's new in v4" release document for a clinic-management app before it's emailed out, using strictly read-only inspection. Per the required skill (`docs-of-record-and-arbitration`), the approach for a customer-facing deliverable is: (1) identify the actual document and its supporting code/manifests, (2) build a fact sheet pairing every claim with concrete evidence from the repo before judging it, (3) give screenshot-heavy content its own integrity check — verify every referenced image exists, every existing image is referenced, and every before/after pair shows the same surface in a comparable state, and (4) check attribution/disclosure text against any stated policy. I set out to answer the four numbered questions strictly from what I could observe in the repository.
